message.content:

Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 17 (28 todo)
Recent changes: The "cursed sword enchantment 5 (both surcharges)" test passed immediately; no implementation change in Green.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion. Keep all tests green. Attempt at least one refactoring or document why none is warranted (brief is fine — no code changed in Green).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
All 17 tests pass. Now let me proceed through the Refactor phase.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

The most recent test, "cursed sword enchantment 5 (both surcharges)", passed without any Green-phase change because the existing `itemSurcharges` already sums all three surcharges. Let me evaluate the names involved.

- `runScenario`, `sumOfBasePremiums`, `sumOfItemSurcharges`, `curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge` — all clearly reveal intent. Good.
- `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` — this name honestly describes a deliberate temporary hack (documented in the comment above it). The name is intentionally awkward to signal its provisional nature. Renaming it would *hide* the smell rather than reveal intent, so I leave it. It will be lifted into a proper policy-wide modifier by the upcoming "10% first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium" test.

Decision: Keep current names. They reveal intent, and the awkward name is load-bearing as a warning.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass (focus area)

The clearest refactoring candidate is `highEnchantmentSurcharge` and `curseSurcharge`, which share a structural pattern (conditional that multiplies item base premium by a rate). But extracting that would couple two distinct business rules through a shared helper — they happen to share shape, not knowledge. That is coincidental, not conceptual, duplication. Forcing DRY here would hurt Rule 2 (intent) and Rule 4 by adding an abstraction that obscures two independent policy rules.

Let me look at a genuinely safe, intent-improving change instead: `itemBasePremium` and the base-premium lookup. `basePremiums[item.type]` is used in `itemBasePremium`, and `basePremiumForGroup` uses `basePremiums[type] * count`.

The cleanest available refactoring: in `itemSurcharges`, the three surcharge functions each independently call `itemBasePremium(item)` and re-read `item.cursed`/`item.enchantment`. There is no harmful duplication of knowledge here — each function owns one rule. Mass is already low and each function is single-purpose.

Let me evaluate one concrete improvement: the comment block + the misleading attachment of first-insurance. The real issue is that `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` is a known temporary hack. Refactoring it now (before the driving test exists) would be implementing ahead — a TDD violation. So that stays.

## Step 3 Evaluation summary (Rules in priority order)

- **Rule 1 (Tests pass):** All 17 green. ✅
- **Rule 2 (Reveals intent):** Names already clear; the one awkward name is intentional. No safe improvement without hiding a documented smell.
- **Rule 3 (No duplication):** The apparent duplication between `curseSurcharge` and `highEnchantmentSurcharge` is *coincidental shape*, not shared knowledge. Extracting it would couple independent rules and reduce clarity. The `itemBasePremium(item)` repetition across surcharge functions is each rule legitimately reading its own input — not knowledge duplication.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest elements):** No unused or over-engineered elements. Each constant/function maps to a spec rule.

## Decision: No code change warranted this cycle

**Refactoring Evaluation:**
- Naming: All names reveal intent; `firstInsuranceSurchargeWhileCursedOnly` is intentionally awkward as a documented, load-bearing warning about a temporary hack — renaming would hide the smell.
- Duplication: The structural similarity between `curseSurcharge` and `highEnchantmentSurcharge` is coincidental shape, not shared knowledge. Extracting a shared helper would couple two independent business rules (Rule 2 and Rule 4 cost > Rule 3 benefit).
- Fewest elements: No dead or speculative code; every element maps to a spec rule.
- The known debt (first-insurance attached to cursed-only) has a dedicated upcoming test ("10% first-insurance surcharge on the policy base premium"). Refactoring it now would be implementing ahead of a test — a TDD violation. The correct moment to lift it is when the Red agent activates that test.

Since Green made no change and the code is already clean and minimal, the most disciplined outcome is to make no code change while documenting the reasoning. Tests remain green.

```
🔄 Refactor Phase Complete:
Refactoring: none performed (documented above)
Mass Change: n/a (no code changed)
Tests: All 17 passing ✅

Proceeding to the next test.
```

The next test to activate is the first `it.todo` in "Quote — policy-wide modifiers": *"should apply 20% loyalty discount for a customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO"* (src/claim-office.spec.ts:219). The parent agent should launch the `red` agent for it.
